coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as a sustainable electrolyte enhancer in aqueous alkaline electrochemical capacitors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Ganesh, Gayathry
Sunil, Vaishak
Ling, JinKiong
Qamar, Ummiya
Misnon, Izan Izwan
Kuila, Biplab Kumar
Das, Santanu
Yang, Chun-Chen
Jose, Rajan
coredata.dc:description: In the endeavour to increase the energy density and to widen the potential window of aqueous alkaline electrochemical capacitors (EC), this study explores the role of carbon dots (CDs) as an additive in potassium hydroxide electrolyte. The CDs with an average size of ∼2.2 nm and negative surface potential are synthesized from a dispersion of palm kernel shell powder in water using a low-temperature hydrothermal process. Electrochemical measurements show that the CD–electrolyte ion (K+) interaction has improved counter ion adsorption in porous carbon electrodes via lowering the characteristic resistances and time constants, which significantly improved the fraction of adsorbed charges than diffusively stored. The improved ionic conductivity is attributed to the improved wettability introduced by the hydrophilic functional groups in the CDs. These parametric changes widened the potential window and marked an 80 % increase in the specific energy and over a 10 % increase in specific power in a practical EC with similar mass-loading as commercial devices. The device with CDs demonstrated superior cycling stability and Coulombic efficiency than the control device without them. These findings underscore the potential of CDs as a promising avenue for advancing the performance parameters of aqueous electrolyte EC, aligning with the overarching goal of realising economical, environmentally friendly, and sustainable energy storage solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Palm kernel shells
Aqueous electrolyte
Supercapacitors
Potential window

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Research & Innovation Department of UMPSA via the grant RDU223101. GG acknowledges financial funding by the Post-graduate Research Scheme (PGRS) through UMP.05.02/26.10/03/03/PGRS2003124, and Institute of Postgraduate Studies through Doctoral Research Scheme (DRS) (reference UMP.20.03/13/13.14/1) and Holistic incentive scheme by UMPSA. GG, RJ and CCY acknowledge Taiwan Ministry of Education for the funding through the Taiwan Experience Education Program (TEEP@AsiaPlus) and Battery Research Centre for Green Energy (BRCGE) of Ming Chi University of Technology (https://www.mcut.edu.my) for the laboratory facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a & b): TEM images of PCD at various magnifications. Inset of (a) shows the SAED pattern showing the amorphous nature of PCD. The inset of (b) shows the average particle size distribution of PCD.

(a) FTIR spectrum representing the functional groups in PCD (b)-(d) Deconvoluted XPS spectrum of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively (e) Schematic representation of various functional groups in the PCD structure.

(a) CV profile of all the samples measured at 5 mV.s−1 (b) GCD curve of all samples recorded at 1 A.g−1 (c) Log i vs log v plot for all samples (d) CV plot of PCD120@KOH at 5 mV.s−1 with the capacitive and diffusive contribution (e) Percentage contribution of all samples at four different scan rates.

(a) EIS data plot for all the samples with inset showing (i) the modelled circuit and (ii) the magnified high-frequency region. Deviation from semicircle in (ii) is due to the limitations in axes selection. (b) Bode plot of all the samples showing the variation of phase angle with increase in frequency (c) Illustration of charge storage mechanism in bare electrolyte and (d) PCD dispersed electrolyte.
(a) The GCD curves at lowest current density, 0.1 A.g−1 (b) EIS spectra with magnified lower frequency region in the inset. (c) Ragone plot (d) Cyclic stability test measured for 5500 at 3 A.g−1.
Compilation of the specific capacitance and electrochemical active surface area in a single electrode setup.
The circuit fitted parameters for bare electrolytes and various concentrations of PCD.
Electrochemical metrics of devices with 1 M KOH and PCD120@KOH.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as a sustainable electrolyte enhancer in aqueous alkaline electrochemical capacitors

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Industrial Sciences and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the endeavour to increase the energy density and to widen the potential window of aqueous alkaline electrochemical capacitors (EC), this study explores the role of carbon dots (CDs) as an additive in potassium hydroxide electrolyte. The CDs with an average size of ∼2.2 nm and negative surface potential are synthesized from a dispersion of palm kernel shell powder in water using a low-temperature hydrothermal process. Electrochemical measurements show that the CD–electrolyte ion (K+) interaction has improved counter ion adsorption in porous carbon electrodes via lowering the characteristic resistances and time constants, which significantly improved the fraction of adsorbed charges than diffusively stored. The improved ionic conductivity is attributed to the improved wettability introduced by the hydrophilic functional groups in the CDs. These parametric changes widened the potential window and marked an 80 % increase in the specific energy and over a 10 % increase in specific power in a practical EC with similar mass-loading as commercial devices. The device with CDs demonstrated superior cycling stability and Coulombic efficiency than the control device without them. These findings underscore the potential of CDs as a promising avenue for advancing the performance parameters of aqueous electrolyte EC, aligning with the overarching goal of realising economical, environmentally friendly, and sustainable energy storage solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The quest for electrical energy storage devices with better charge storability and deliverability from materials of sustainable resources has gained significant attention recently as an effort to confront climate change. Traditional battery technologies that dominate the market face challenges in meeting the requirements of high-power applications, long cycle life, and rapid charge-discharge capabilities. Furthermore, conventional electrode and electrolyte materials in present-day batteries lack sustainability and create several socioeconomic problems [1–4]. In this context, electrochemical capacitors, synonymously, supercapacitors (SCs), have emerged as a promising solution due to their capability of delivering high power density and long life spans using sustainable materials such as carbon. However, their relatively lower energy density than conventional batteries has been a crucial factor limiting their widespread adoption in numerous applications requiring both high power and energy densities [5,6].

The choice of a suitable electrolyte plays a pivotal role in determining the operational potential window, which directly affects the achievable energy density. Among the wide variety of electrolytes, aqueous electrolytes hold the upper hand by their lower toxicity, non-flammability, lower viscosity, higher ionic conductivity, lower internal resistance in comparison to organic electrolytes, and lack of current collector corrosion [7,8]. However, the potential window of aqueous electrolytes is limited by the water electrolysis occurring at 1.23 V, which prevents these electrolytes from achieving higher voltages and energy densities [9–11]. Despite this demerit, lower cost, safety and ease of handling in ambience motivate investigations on novel strategies to enhance charge storability and energy density of aqueous electrolytes by suitable additives [8,12]. In this pursuit, the inclusion of small quantities of redox additives is widely studied and has demonstrated notable high performances in aqueous electrolytes [13,14]. Yet, they come with several drawbacks such as moderate cyclic stability due to strong redox reactions at the electrode/electrolyte interface [15,16], large leakage current and high self-discharge at low current densities [17,18], limited solubility and large molecular sizes that adversely affect the mass transport within the device [19,20].

Carbon dots (CDs) are quantum-sized structures of <10 nm that have found applications in diverse fields like biomedicine, optoelectronics, catalysis, and sensors [21,22]. A typical CD structure comprises a sp2 hybridised carbon inner core and a sp3 hybridised outer core enriched with surface moieties of heteroatoms [23,24]. These surface dangling bonds on CDs can positively contribute to the electrochemical activity, due to which the prospects of CDs have expanded to energy storage technologies such as SCs [25]. In a previous study, an egg yolk-derived carbon dot-modified SC electrode enabled a voltage window expansion from 1 V in a three-electrode system to 1.8 V in a two-electrode device in 6 M KOH electrolyte, resulting in a specific energy of ∼51.4 Wh.kg‐ 1 at a specific power of ∼450 W.kg−1 [26]. In the same study, an impressive voltage window of 2.6 V voltage was attained in 1.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte due to the suppression of hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions and delivered a specific energy of ∼107.5 Wh.kg−1 at a specific power of ∼650 W.kg−1. The expansion of the voltage window and performance improvement is also seen in a different study of a hybrid SC with CDs incorporated into hydrogel-derived carbon electrodes [27]. The improved voltage window is due to the strong affinity exhibited by the functional groups on the CD surface towards the cations in the electrolyte and also due to the inhibition of the hydrogen evolution reaction [27]. To the best of our knowledge, the effect of CDs as an additive in aqueous electrolytes has not been studied before. Nevertheless, Paoprasert et al. explored CDs derived from rice husk [28], cassava peels [29], and sugarcane bagasse [30] in gel polymer electrolytes with a focus on developing bio-renewable SCs. Subsequently, a specific capacitance of ∼491 F.g−1 in rice-based starch-H3PO4, ∼375 F.g−1 with cassava starch-H2SO4, and ∼456 F.g−1 molasses-H2SO4 gel-polymer electrolyte were achieved. These studies showcased the potential of applying CDs as additives to enhance the electrochemical performance of aqueous electrolytes. The surface dangling bonds of the CDs could positively interact with the electrode or conductive ions within the electrolyte, enhancing the electrochemical performance of the overall device. Moreover, dispersing CDs is much more sustainable and environmentally friendly than using other chemical additives.

This study attempts to elucidate the electrochemical properties of CDs when utilized as an aqueous electrolyte additive in 1 M KOH for SC applications. The CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal technique and were characterized in terms of their physicochemical properties. Varying concentrations of CDs were incorporated into the 1 M KOH electrolyte to correlate its role in the enhancement of the electrochemical performance of the porous carbon electrode. Suitable circuit models were used to fit the impedance spectra to decode the underlying mechanism of the enhanced charge storage in CD-based systems. A symmetric device in the carbon//CDs@KOH//carbon configuration widened the potential window from 1.6 V to 1.8 V and an 80 % increase in specific energy. These findings underscore the potential of CDs as a promising avenue for advancing the performance parameters of aqueous electrolyte supercapacitors, aligning with the overarching goal of realising economical, environmentally friendly, and sustainable energy storage solutions.

The CDs for the present studies were sourced from palm kernel shells (PKS) from a local farm in Malaysia. The commercial activated carbon for SCs, FCO-20 was purchased from Zhenjiang Forest Co. Ltd. (specific area = ∼2200 m2g−1, average size pore size = ∼3.5 nm); carbon black from Imerys Graphite & Carbon, Switzerland; poly (vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF, MW ∼ 530,000 pellets by GPC) from Aldrich, China; N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99 %) and potassium hydroxide (KOH, ≥85 % basis, pellets) was purchased from R&M Chemicals.
(a)
Synthesis of PKS-derived CD

In this study, PKS-derived CDs (PCDs) were synthesized via the hydrothermal method as reported previously [31]. In general, the process involved washing the PKS several times in deionized water and drying overnight at ∼80 °C. The PKS was then finely powdered with a mesh size of 0.75 mm and stored for use as the precursor for PCD synthesis. To synthesize PCD, 8 g of PKS powder was stirred with 50 ml of DI water for 30 min. The mixture was then transferred to a 200 ml Teflon-lined steel autoclave and kept in an oven for 14 h at ∼160 °C to obtain PCD. The as-synthesized PCD solution was allowed to cool down naturally and was then filtered to obtain a light, brown-coloured solution. The solution was then centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 1 h to sediment and remove the larger particles. The supernatant was collected and stored in a refrigerator at 5 °C before usage.
(b)
Material characterizations and electrochemical characterizations

The morphology and particle size of the PCD were analysed using Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TECNAI G2 20 TWIN, FEI-USA). The sample was drop-casted on a carbon-coated 200 mesh TEM grid (SPi Supplies, USA). The crystal structure of the PCD was studied using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker/D2 Phaser) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) in 2θ = 5–60° diffraction range. Confocal-Raman microscope (model α-300 access, WITec GmBH, Germany, presently, Oxford Instruments, UK) with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm and 600 gratings was used for all the Raman spectroscopy measurements. All the Raman measurements were performed under the equivalent conditions of constant laser power of ∼1.5 mW. Fourier transmission infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, PerkinElmer 580B spectrophotometer, equipped with a PerkinElmer 3600 IR data station, Germany) was used to investigate the chemical structure of PCD. The surface chemistry was further confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), by using a spectrometer (Model: K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) using Al kα as the X-ray source. All the XPS data analysis and peak fitting were performed using Gaussian-Lorentzian fit with XPSPEAK 4.1 software. The Zeta potential of the dilute aqueous solution of PCD (pH ∼ 7) was measured using Zetasizer Ultra (ZSU5700), Malvern Analytical (UK) instrument. The contact angle measurements were conducted by placing a droplet of 1 M KOH and PCD120@KOH on the FCO-20 electrode surface using a contact angle goniometer (MagicDroplet, Model 100SB, USA). Finally, the half-cell and full-cell electrochemical performances were studied using a potentiostat-galvanostat (PGSTAT M101, Metrohm Autolab B.V., The Netherlands) using NOVA 2.1.4 software. All the electrochemical characterisations were carried out at 25 °C.
(c)
Preparation of electrode and electrolyte

The working electrodes were prepared by mixing FCO-20 (∼20 mg), conductive carbon black (2.5 mg), and PVDF binder (35 μL of 7 wt% PVDF dispersed in NMP solution) in a ratio of 80: 10:10 wt%. The powder was dispersed in 0.2 mL of NMP and stirred overnight at room temperature. The prepared slurry was then coated on graphite foil with an active area of 1 cm2 and a mass loading of ∼3 mg, followed by oven drying for 3 h at 80 °C. For the preparation of aqueous electrolyte, 8 mL of 1 M KOH was uniformly mixed with various concentrations of PCD (60, 120, and 180 mg.mL−1, which is nearly equivalent to 0.5, 1 and 1.5 ml of PCD solution), and are named PCD60@KOH, PCD120@KOH and PCD180@KOH. The total amount of solid (KOH) of the resultant electrolyte is maintained at 1 M throughout the study. For this, 1.11 M KOH solution is prepared initially, and the solution is made up to 1 M by diluting with 0.5, 1, and 1.5 ml of PCD solution to obtain PCD@KOH of various concentrations. For full device fabrication, the slurry prepared was blade coated on a pre-cleaned and sulphuric acid etched copper foil. The electrodes were then dried in an oven at 80 °C overnight and further heated at 120 °C for 1 h to remove all solvent. The prepared electrodes were then cut to a disc shape with a diameter of 1.5 cm. The reference device was fabricated using 0.3 ml of bare electrolyte (1 M KOH), compared with a device prepared using the optimized electrolyte of the same quantity. The full devices were fabricated in a Swagelok setup with glass fibre as the separator. The electrochemical performance was recorded using the multi-channel potentiostat/galvanostat (Autolab workstation with M101 module FRA32M attachment, Metrohm). Different scan rates of potentiostatic cyclic voltammetry (CV) were analysed over a voltage range of −0.1 to 0 V vs Ag/AgCl in the half-cell study and 0 to 1.8 V in the symmetric cell study. Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) data were recorded over the same voltage range as that of CV for various current densities, and lastly, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data were recorded across a frequency range of 10 mHz to 1 MHz at an amplitude of 10 mV. The calculation details for specific capacitance, specific energy and specific power are provided in Supplementary Note 1 (Supporting information).

The morphology of the as-synthesized PCD was investigated using TEM, where the particle size distribution of the PCD was estimated using ImageJ software. The TEM images were captured at different magnifications, as depicted in Fig. 1(a and b). A diffused ring can be observed from the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) analysis (see inset of Fig. 1(a)), indicating the amorphous nature of as-synthesized PCDs. A similar amorphous phase can also be observed as the broad diffraction peak at 2θ ∼21° in the XRD spectrum and the low ID/IG ratio of 0.71 in Raman analysis (see Supporting information Fig. S1(a) and (b)). Further, Fig. 1(b) shows the spheroidal particles of PCD with a size distribution between ∼2–3 nm and an average particle size of approximately ∼2.3 nm, as presented in the inset of Fig. 1(b).
(b)
Chemical properties of PCD

To investigate the presence of surface functional groups, FTIR analysis was performed (see Fig. 2(a)). The PCD shows stretching vibrational peaks of –OH at 3312 cm−1, –CH at 2885 cm−1, –C=O/C=N at 1663 cm−1, –NH at 1396 cm−1 and –C–O–C at 1106 cm−1, as well as –OH in-phase bending vibration at 1282 cm−1. The presence of these oxygen and nitrogen-containing surface functional groups could potentially aid in improving the electrochemical performance of SCs by introducing pseudocapacitance [32–34]. The surface chemical elemental composition and oxidation state were further examined using XPS, revealing two predominant peaks of C 1s, and O 1s, together with a small peak of N 1s in the survey scan (see Supporting information Fig. S2). The deconvolution of the C1s narrow scan spectrum in Fig. 2(b) showed three deconvoluted peaks at 284.18, 285.58 and 287.69 eV, corresponding to –C–C/C=C, –C–O and –C=O bonds, respectively [35–37]. Further, the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2(c)) can be deconvoluted into peaks corresponding to the pyrrolic N (N5) bond at 399.38 eV and quaternary nitrogen (N-Q) at 401.28 eV [38]. The presence of pyrrolic N structure suggests PCD can alter the charge carrier density helping to produce larger interface capacitance [25]. Finally, the deconvoluted peaks at 530.88 and 532.28 eV in the O 1s spectrum in Fig. 2(d) confirm –C=O (quinone) and C–OH (phenol) bonds, respectively [9,39,40]. Fig. 2(e) shows the schematic illustration of the chemical structure of PCDs.
(c)
Electrochemical analyses
(i)
Single electrode-electrolyte interface (three–electrode system)

The effect of dispersing PCD60, PCD120 and PCD180 in 1 M KOH on the electrochemical performance of porous carbon was studied from −1 V to 0 V (the LSV data is provided in the Supporting information S2(b)) using the typical three-electrode system measurement. The capacitive nature of the electrodes was evaluated using CV plots depicted in Fig. 3(a). The CV profile of FCO-20 in 1 M KOH showed a nearly rectangular shape, which is typical of the electric double-layer capacitance (EDLC) charge storage mechanism. On the other hand, the CV profiles of PCD@KOH demonstrate a minor deviation from the ideal EDLC profile at lower scan rates. Upon increasing the scan rate, the CV curve exhibits an oblique parallelogram due to rapid charge transfer with lower series resistance [41,42]. The CV profiles suggest that the addition of PCDs to the electrolyte enhance the charge storability of the electrodes. CV curves of all the samples at different scan rates are available in Supporting information Fig. S3. From Fig. 3(b), the GCD profile of FCO-20 in bare electrolyte demonstrates a quasi-triangular shape, whereas PCD@KOH samples exhibited greater deviation from the ideal triangular shape. PCD120@KOH shows almost a 2-fold improvement in specific capacitance compared to the bare electrolyte (∼192 F.g−1), delivering a specific capacitance of ∼400 F.g−1. The specific capacitances of all the samples are given in Table 1 . Evidently, an optimum concentration of PCD delivers the best performance. A lower concentration of PCD in the electrolyte would have a lesser influence in providing sufficient redox active species for capacitance improvement, while an excess amount might flocculate and block the pores of the electrode material, leading to a lowering of capacitance. It is observed that the electrodes tested in PCD@KOH showed higher rate capabilities in contrast to the bare, due to the beneficial contribution of oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on PCDs to the existing EDLC charge storage mechanism [43,44]. The rate capability of all the samples was evaluated by measuring the GCD at various current densities are shown in Fig. S4 and their values are provided in Table S1 of Supporting information. The potential drop between the charge and discharge curves which mainly arises from the electrolyte resistance and the contact resistance that occurs at the electrode-substrate interface is described as the equivalent series resistance (ESR). The ESR of the bare electrolyte is ∼4.1 Ω, which drops to ∼3.2 Ω for PCD120@KOH, which indicates the increased ion conductivity with PCD addition. The ESR values of all the samples are given in Table S2 in Supporting information.

To understand the respective contribution of current from surface-controlled and diffusion-controlled processes, the power-law relationship between peak current and scan rate was analysed using [26,45]:
(1)
log
i = log
a + blog
v
where i is the peak current response, v is the scan rate, and a and b are adjustable parameters. The slope of the linear plot between the log i and the log v values determines the b value which suggests whether the charge storage is surface-controlled dominant (b close to 1) or diffusion-control dominant (b close to 0.5). From Fig. 3(c), the b value of the anodic current response for 1 M KOH raised from 0.64 to 0.98 with the addition of PCD, indicating that the addition of PCD has significantly improved the surface-controlled mechanism. To further evaluate the capacitive (k1) and diffusion (k2) contribution at a scan rate (v), Dunn's equation was adopted [46,47]:
(2)
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2

Fig. 3(d) shows the percentage contribution of capacitive (∼63 %) and diffusion (37 %) controlled mechanism of PCD120@KOH at 5 mV.s−1. Fig. 3(e) consolidates the percentage contributions for all four samples at 5, 20, 60 and 100 mV.s−1. In PCD, the capacitive contribution increases at both lower and higher scan rates. At 100 mV.s−1, while the capacitive contribution in the bare electrolyte was about 66 %, the addition of PCD increased it to nearly 90 % for all the samples, explaining the enhancement in rate capability.

The Nyquist plots in Fig. 4(a) were evaluated to gain insights into the complex impedances associated with charge adsorption, diffusion, and charge transfer in the system. In a typical Nyquist plot, the diameter of the semi-circle in the high-frequency region defines the charge transfer resistance (Rct), linear tail at lower frequency corresponding to the Warburg impedance (W), which represents the ion diffusion into the electrode material and the intercept of the semi-circle with the real axis gives the internal resistance (Rs), which cumulates the resistances encountered by the ions across various interfaces like the bulk electrolyte, electrode, and current collector [48–51]. Further, modelling an equivalent circuit for the Nyquist plots using Z-view software with real-world components like resistors and capacitors, helps in elucidating the underlying charge storage mechanism. The goodness of fit of the circuit model is shown in Fig. S5 of Supporting information. To briefly describe the circuit (inset (i) of Fig. 4(a)), the Rs is given in series to a capacitor (Cdl) that represents the double layer at the electrode-electrolyte interface. To accommodate the resistance involved during the transference of charge from the electrolyte ions to the electrode, a charge transfer resistor (Rct) is introduced parallel to Cdl. As the kinetics of the faradaic reaction is related to the diffusion of charges into the electrode material, a diffusional resistance is introduced using Warburg impedance (ZW) in series with Rct. The assessment of low-frequency component ZW encompasses ZW-R (diffusion resistance) and ZW-T (diffusion time constant). Attributable to several factors including heterogeneous charge distribution across the electrode surface, material heterogeneities, electrode porosity and lower rates of adsorption, Cdl is replaced using CPEd is known as the constant phase element, and its impedance at a given frequency is defined as ZCPE = 1/C(jω)α [52,53]. The CPEd component accounts for the non-ideal behaviour at the electrode/electrolyte interface. It is contingent upon the ‘C’ value, associated with the deviations from the ideal capacitive behaviour and the ‘α’ value, a dimensionless parameter decisive in determining the capacitive or resistive nature of the system. The values of all the mentioned electrical components are provided in Table 2 . As can be seen from the table, the Rs value significantly drops with the addition of PCD into the electrolyte from ∼4.3 Ω to ∼1 Ω. This decrement shows that PCDs increase the ionic conductivity while reducing the resistance and can be attributed to the improved wettability introduced by the hydrophilic functional groups. Similarly, the resistance of transferring charges from the electrolyte ions to the pores of the electrode material is measured highest (0.35 Ω) for the bare and reduced to 0.24 Ω with PCD120 addition. This reduction observed in the Rct value implies that the addition of PCDs increases the charge transfer by shortening the ionic pathway via the surface functionalities and improving ionic conductivity [54,55]. For PCD@KOH systems, the CPEd-C value is negligible, indicating that they resemble an ideal capacitive behaviour. This claim can be further validated by the α value, which is close to 0.8 for all PCD@KOH. Further, ZW-R exhibits a minimum value for the bare electrode and reaches its maximum upon the addition of 120 mg.mL−1 PCD. Conversely, ZW-T shows an inverse correlation. Finally, CPEpc denotes the capacitance arising from voltage-dependent faradaic phenomena, while R1 corresponds to self-discharge dynamics [51].

To understand the phase variation of impedance with frequency, a bode phase plot was drawn as shown in Fig. 4(b). Ideally, SCs show capacitive behaviour at a phase angle close to −90° at low frequencies and a decline in the phase angle to 0° at higher frequencies, as the system becomes more resistive [48]. At an angle of −45°, the capacitive and resistive parameters of the circuit become comparable and the frequency at this point is considered as the characteristic frequency (f). As can be seen in Fig. 4(b), the addition of PCD into the system increased the phase angle from <60° to ∼80°, implying that the PCDs improved the capacitive behaviour of the system, which is consistent with Dunn's analysis and the ‘α’ value. The relaxation time (τ) which is the inverse of characteristic frequency, represents the shortest duration required for complete discharge with an efficiency >50 % [56,57]. The relaxation time of 2.71, 1.60, 0.45 and 0.20 s are calculated for bare, PCD180, PCD60 and PCD120@KOH, respectively. The lowering of the relaxation time arises from the improved conductivity, which enables efficient charge transport and from the improved diffusion of ions into the pores of the electrode. The lower relaxation time observed for the added system is also responsible for the higher rate capability and lower Rct [58,59].

The experimental results unequivocally demonstrate the beneficial effects of introducing PCD into a 1 M KOH electrolyte on the electrochemical performance of carbon/carbon symmetric devices. A possible underlying charge storage mechanism for this improved performance is illustrated in Fig. 4(c) and (d). Briefly, the higher surface-to-volume ratio of nanostructures like PCD with an average size of 2.3 nm provides a large surface area for enhanced ion adsorption. Notably, the average pore size of the FCO-20 is 3.5 nm, which shows that PCDs could also easily diffuse into the pores of the electrode material. The existence of functional groups on the surface of PCD results in a net negative surface of −23.25 mV (refer to Fig. S6(a) of Supporting information) which efficiently separates charges and increases counter ion adsorption. Moreover, the electronegative surface promotes these nanostructures to anchor onto the surface and pores of the electrode material. Such anchorage could lead to a significant increment of ∼105 % in the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) (SE in Table 1) of PCD120@KOH for ion adsorption, which leads to increased specific capacitance. Furthermore, the incomplete wetting of activated carbon electrodes when immersed in aqueous electrolytes results in inefficient pore access for the ions (refer to Fig. S6(b) in Supporting information where the contact angle measured showed θ = 87°) [60,61]. This elevates the Rs and Rct values. The incorporation of PCDs with enriched oxygen (especially, the phenolic, hydroxyl and carbonyl) and nitrogen-bearing functional groups enhances the wettability inside the pores (refer to Fig. S6(c) in Supporting information where the contact angle measured showed θ = 59°), culminating in a reduction of the resistances and improving the capacitance [33,62,63]. Additionally, the lowering of diffusional resistance and diffusion time also implies that the functional groups aided ion diffusivity into the pores of the electrode material. Thus, PCDs that diffuse into the pores could also act as a bridge or a conductive network for the charge transfer from the ions to the pores through the following possible reactions [64]:

ce:list-item.ce:label: (ii)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Charge storage in a symmetric supercapacitor

ce:section.ce:para: To validate the performance in practical applications, symmetric SCs were assembled with 1 M KOH as the control device and PCD120@KOH as the test device, with active electrode mass loadings of ∼7.3 mg. The comparison of various electrochemical metrics is depicted in Fig. 5 and the data are summarised in Table 3 . Fig. S7(a) and (b) in Supporting information illustrate CV curves recorded at 10 mV.s−1 for varying voltages and Fig. S7(c) in Supplementary information shows the CV recorded at optimized voltages. The quasi-rectangular shape of the CV profile of the control device exhibits a potential window of 1.6 V, while the addition of PCDs extended the voltage window up to 1.8 V. This improved potential window signifies that the test device approaches the expected 2 V potential window. The corresponding GCD curves corroborate the CV plot by attaining the potential window, as can be seen in Fig. 5(a). The specific capacitance measured for the control and PCD-based device at 0.1 A.g−1 is ∼29 F.g−1 and ∼41 F.g−1. The GCD curves plotted for various current densities from 0.1 to 3 A.g−1 show excellent rate capabilities compared to the control (see Supporting information Fig. S8). The voltage window expansion observed upon PCD incorporation could potentially be attributed to the lowering of internal resistances and mitigation of hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions of the electrolyte [26].

The Nyquist plot for the full devices is presented in Fig. 5(b). The magnified image of the low-frequency region provided in the inset of Fig. 5(b) clearly shows a significant decrease in Rs with the addition of PCD. While the control device showed Rs of 2.9 Ω, the contribution of PCDs lowered the Rs to 1.6 Ω. A similar decrement is also seen in the Rct value, where the values dropped from 1.1 to 0.5 Ω. The dependence of complex capacitance on frequency was assessed using capacitance vs. frequency plots for both devices to evaluate the actual capacitance and energy dissipation. Fig. S9(a) (Supporting information) shows the variation of real capacitance (C′) which represents the conventional capacitor behaviour at low frequencies, or the energy stored at the double layer. In the same frequency region, PCD-based devices can store more charge. Fig. S9(b) (Supporting information) shows the behaviour of the imaginary part (C″) of capacitance with varying frequency and encompasses the capacitive dispersion [65,66]. The τ value is calculated for both devices and is obtained as 5.68 s for the control device and 0.43 s for the test device. This noticeable decrease in the relaxation time shows higher rates of ionic diffusion. Additionally, the device acts as a capacitor until a frequency of τ−1, above which it becomes resistive.

The superior performance of the test device is clear from the Ragone plot shown in Fig. 5(c). The bare KOH device delivered a maximum specific energy of ∼10 Wh.kg−1 and a maximum specific power of 2400 W.kg−1. The addition of PCD enhances the performance of the device by recording a maximum specific energy of ∼18.5 Wh.kg−1 and a maximum power of 2700 W.kg−1. The lower resistance values could be attributed to this simultaneous increase in specific energy and specific power. Even at a higher current density (3 A.g−1), the device managed to deliver a specific energy of ∼12 Wh.kg−1 with low potential drop (refer Fig S10(a) and (b) in the Supporting information). The performance proves the viability of the SC device in practical applications. Further, the addition of PCDs led to a capacitance retention of 108 % after 5500 cycles, while the bare electrolyte retained only about 78 %, as shown in Fig. 5(d). Similarly, the bare electrolyte showed a degradation of 87 % in Coulombic efficiency, while the PCD addition maintained a Coulombic efficiency of ∼93 % throughout 5500 cycles, which shows the minimal loss and electrochemical cycling stability introduced upon PCD addition.

In conclusion, this study demonstrated a sustainable and economically viable strategy of utilizing carbon dots (CDs) as additives to KOH-based electrolyte to increase the electrochemical performance of aqueous supercapacitors. The hydrothermal synthesis using a dispersion of a biowaste (palm kernel shell) powder in water yielded CDs of a dimension of ∼2–3 nm, endowed with hydrophilic functional groups which contributed to a net negative surface of ∼−23 mV. Under applied bias, the surface polarity aided CDs to anchor on the electrode surface and facilitate additional electrochemical active sites for ion adsorption. With these interactions, in a single electrode measurement setup, an addition of 120 mg.mL−1 of CD doubled the specific capacitance, accompanied by an increased capacitive charge storage. The improved wettability introduced by the hydrophilic functional groups further reduced resistances and time constants. These interactions aided in expanding the potential window and in increasing the specific energy by 80 % and specific power by over 10 % in a practical electrochemical capacitor with similar mass-loading as commercial devices. Notably, the CD@KOH device showed superior cycling stability and Coulombic efficiency than the control device, highlighting the potential of CDs as effective additives for advancing the performance of aqueous supercapacitors.

Gayathry Ganesh: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Vaishak Sunil: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. JinKiong Ling: Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis. Ummiya Qamar: Validation, Data curation. Izan Izwan Misnon: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Formal analysis. Biplab Kumar Kuila: Validation, Data curation. Santanu Das: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Chun-Chen Yang: Validation, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Rajan Jose: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
